name: Mach Number (Ma)
desc: Dimensionless ratio of local flow speed (V) to the speed of sound (c) in that fluid.
validations: 📌 Sonic flow (Ma = 1), Subsonic (Ma < 1), Supersonic (Ma > 1).
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Don't assume gases are ALWAYS compressible! If Ma < 0.3 (like air under 100 m/s), density changes are < 5% and the gas MUST be treated as an INCOMPRESSIBLE fluid.

name: Specific Weight (γ)
desc: The weight of a unit volume of a substance.
validations: 📌 Standard gravity 'g' is approx 9.81 m/s² at sea level.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Density (ρ) is constant, but Specific Weight (γ) changes with location because 'g' changes! Never confuse it with Specific Gravity.

name: No-Slip Condition
desc: A fluid in direct contact with a solid surface 'sticks' to it due to viscous effects, causing the boundary layer.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: The absolute velocity of the fluid isn't always zero! The RELATIVE velocity is zero. If a plate is moving at 5 m/s, the fluid particle exactly at the wall is ALSO moving at 5 m/s.

name: Dimensional Homogeneity
desc: All additive terms in an engineering equation must have the exact same primary dimensions.
validations: 📌 Used strictly to catch errors in formula derivation.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: You CANNOT add standard Energy (kJ) with Specific Energy (kJ/kg) directly! Always multiply the specific term by mass (m) first before adding. (Classic silly mistake in Thermodynamics/Fluid equations).

name: Newton's Unity Conversion (Force)
desc: The fundamental conversion ratios for force in SI and English systems.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: In the English system, never cancel lbf with lbm directly! You MUST use the conversion factor gc = 32.174 (lbm·ft)/(lbf·s²) to balance the units.

name: Ideal Gas Equation of State
desc: Relates absolute pressure, density, and absolute temperature for a gas.
validations: 📌 R is the specific gas constant (Universal Gas Constant / Molar Mass).
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Temperature MUST be in Kelvin (or Rankine) and Pressure MUST be Absolute! Never use Gauge pressure or Celsius here.

name: Coefficient of Compressibility (Bulk Modulus, K)
desc: Measures a fluid's resistance to compression. Higher K means more incompressible.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: For an IDEAL GAS, the isothermal bulk modulus K is exactly equal to its absolute pressure (K = P). Don't waste time deriving it in the exam!

name: Coefficient of Volume Expansion (β)
desc: Measures how much density changes with temperature at constant pressure.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: For an IDEAL GAS, β = 1/T. Students often put T in Celsius here. It MUST be in Kelvin! If T = 27°C, β = 1/300 K⁻¹, NOT 1/27.

name: Newton's Law of Viscosity
desc: Relates shear stress to the velocity gradient (rate of shear strain) for Newtonian fluids.
validations: 📌 μ is Dynamic Viscosity (Pa·s). 1 Poise = 0.1 Pa·s.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Temperature effect is a classic MSQ! Liquid viscosity DECREASES with temp (cohesive forces weaken). Gas viscosity INCREASES with temp (molecular collisions increase).

name: Kinematic Viscosity (ν)
desc: Ratio of dynamic viscosity to density. Represents momentum diffusivity.
validations: 📌 Unit is m²/s. 1 Stoke (St) = 1 cm²/s = 10⁻⁴ m²/s.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: For gases, dynamic viscosity (μ) is mostly independent of pressure, but kinematic viscosity (ν) DEPENDS strongly on pressure because density (ρ) changes!

name: Pressure Jump Across Curved Surface
desc: The pressure difference between the inside and outside of a curved liquid interface.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: A soap 'bubble' has TWO surfaces (inner and outer), hence 4σ/R. A liquid 'droplet' only has ONE surface, hence 2σ/R. Also, the formula uses Radius (R), examiners often give Diameter (D) to trick you.

name: Capillary Rise/Fall (h)
desc: The elevation or depression of a liquid in a narrow tube due to surface tension.
validations: 📌 ϕ is the contact angle.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: If the contact angle ϕ > 90° (like Mercury in glass), cos(ϕ) is negative, resulting in a Capillary FALL (depression). Always double-check if the question gave 'Diameter' instead of 'Radius' (R)!

name: Cavitation Condition
desc: Formation of destructive vapor bubbles when local static pressure in a flowing liquid drops below its vapor pressure.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Cavitation is driven by PRESSURE drops (e.g., in pump impellers or venturi throats), not just temperature increases. It causes severe structural pitting when the bubbles collapse.

name: Manometry & Pressure Drop
desc: Calculates pressure difference using a fluid column. Pressure increases going DOWN, decreases going UP.
validations: 📌 For gas flowing in a pipe, ρ_fluid is negligible compared to ρ_manometer.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Always jump across the U-tube at the SAME horizontal fluid level to equate pressure. Never equate pressures across two DIFFERENT fluids at the same level!

name: Hydrostatic Force on Plane Surface
formula: F_R = P_c * A = (P_0 + ρ * g * h_c) * A
desc: Total force exerted by a static fluid on a submerged flat plate.
validations: 📌 h_c is the vertical depth to the CENTROID of the plate.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: The resultant force depends ONLY on the pressure at the CENTROID (h_c), NOT the center of pressure! Do not confuse the two.

name: Center of Pressure (y_p)
desc: The exact point where the resultant hydrostatic force acts on a submerged surface.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: The center of pressure (y_p) is ALWAYS below the centroid (y_c). If atmospheric pressure acts on both sides of the plate, it cancels out—so use Gage Pressure (P_0 = 0) in the formula!

name: Hydrostatic Force on Curved Surface
formula: F_H = Force on vertical projection  |  F_V = Force on horizontal projection ± Weight of fluid
desc: Breaks the force on a curved surface into horizontal and vertical components.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: For F_V, the 'Weight' is the weight of the fluid block DIRECTLY ABOVE the curved surface. If the liquid is completely BELOW the curved surface (pushing up), the vertical force acts UPWARDS.

name: Buoyant Force (Archimedes' Principle)
desc: Upward force exerted by a fluid equal to the weight of the displaced fluid.
validations: 📌 For a floating body, F_B exactly equals the total weight of the body.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: ALWAYS use the density of the FLUID (ρ_fluid), NOT the solid body! And V_submerged is only the portion under the fluid level, not the total volume.

name: Fluid in Rigid-Body Linear Acceleration
desc: Calculates the angle (θ) of the free liquid surface when a container accelerates.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Pay strict attention to a_z (vertical acceleration). If the tank accelerates UPWARD, use (g + a_z). If it accelerates DOWNWARD, use (g - a_z). In free fall (a_z = -g), hydrostatic pressure variation becomes ZERO!

name: Forced Vortex (Rotating Cylinder)
desc: Calculates the maximum height difference between the edge and the center of a rotating liquid.
validations: 📌 The free surface forms a paraboloid of revolution.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: The angular velocity (ω) MUST be in rad/s, not RPM! Multiply RPM by (2π/60) before putting it in the formula. Also, the volume of the empty paraboloid space is exactly HALF the volume of a cylinder with the same dimensions.

name: Material (Total) Acceleration
desc: Total acceleration of a fluid particle = Local Acceleration (time) + Convective Acceleration (space).
validations: 📌 For steady flow, the local acceleration (∂u/∂t) is zero.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: A fluid particle CAN accelerate even in a purely STEADY flow! (e.g., flow through a converging nozzle). Students often mistakenly assume a = 0 just because the flow is steady.

name: Streamline Equation (2D/3D)
formula: dx / u = dy / v = dz / w
desc: Mathematical equation for a curve that is everywhere tangent to the instantaneous local velocity vector.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Streamlines, Pathlines, and Streaklines are ONLY identical in STEADY flow. If the velocity field has a 't' in it (Unsteady Flow), they are all completely different curves!

name: Continuity Equation (Incompressible Flow)
desc: The volumetric strain rate must be exactly zero for an incompressible flow field.
validations: 📌 In 2D, this reduces to (∂u/∂x) + (∂v/∂y) = 0.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Incompressibility does NOT mean velocity is constant. It just means whatever goes into a volume must come out. Always check (∂u/∂x) + (∂v/∂y) = 0 to verify if a given 2D velocity profile is physically possible!

name: Vorticity Vector (ζ)
desc: Vorticity is the curl of the velocity vector and is exactly twice the angular velocity of the fluid particle.
validations: 📌 If ζ = 0 everywhere, the flow is strictly IRROTATIONAL.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Don't confuse Vorticity with Shear Strain! Vorticity in 2D is (∂v/∂x - ∂u/∂y). It has a MINUS sign. A positive value means the fluid particle is spinning counter-clockwise.

name: Angular Velocity (2D xy-plane)
desc: Average rotation rate of two initially perpendicular lines intersecting at a point.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Students often forget the 0.5 factor! Vorticity is without the 0.5, but Angular Velocity REQUIRES the 0.5 multiplier.

name: Reynolds Transport Theorem (RTT) - Steady Flow
desc: Relates the rate of change of a system property to the net flux across a Control Volume boundary.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: In the integral form (ρb V·n dA), the dot product V·n dictates the sign. Outflow is POSITIVE (vectors in same direction), Inflow is NEGATIVE (vectors oppose). Forgetting this sign convention ruins the entire mass/energy balance!

name: Continuity Equation (Incompressible)
desc: Conservation of mass for steady, incompressible flow in a single stream.
validations: 📌 Volume flow rate (V_dot) is conserved ONLY for incompressible fluids.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Never use A1V1 = A2V2 for GASES at high speeds! For compressible flow, you MUST include density: ρ1*A1*V1 = ρ2*A2*V2. Many students lose 2 marks ignoring density changes.

name: Bernoulli Equation
desc: Conservation of mechanical energy for steady, incompressible, frictionless flow along a streamline.
validations: 📌 Each term represents 'Head' (Energy per unit weight) in meters.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Do NOT apply Bernoulli across a pump, a turbine, or through a boundary layer! It assumes ZERO friction and ZERO shaft work. If a machine is present, you must use the General Energy Equation.

name: Stagnation Pressure (Pitot Tube)
desc: Calculates fluid velocity using the difference between stagnation and static pressure.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Pay strict attention to the density (ρ) in the denominator. It must be the density of the FLOWING FLUID (e.g., Air), NOT the density of the manometer fluid (e.g., Water or Mercury) used to measure the pressure difference!

name: EGL and HGL (Grade Lines)
desc: EGL represents total head; HGL represents pressure + elevation head.
validations: 📌 EGL is always physically higher than HGL by the velocity head.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: EGL always drops in the direction of flow due to friction. However, HGL can actually RISE if the pipe diameter increases (since velocity drops, kinetic energy converts back to pressure).

name: General Energy Equation (Head Form)
formula: (P1/ρg) + α1(V1²/2g) + z1 + h_pump = (P2/ρg) + α2(V2²/2g) + z2 + h_turbine + h_L
desc: The complete mechanical energy balance accounting for machines and friction losses.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Do not forget the Kinetic Energy Correction Factor (α)! For fully developed turbulent flow, α ≈ 1.05 (often ignored), but for fully developed LAMINAR flow, α = 2.0. Missing this completely ruins the calculation.

name: Pump & Turbine Efficiency
desc: Calculates how effectively mechanical energy is transferred between the machine and the fluid.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: The formulas are inverses of each other! For a pump, fluid energy is the output (numerator). For a turbine, shaft work is the output (numerator). Never mix these up.

name: Steady Linear Momentum Equation
formula: ΣF = Σ(β * m_dot * V)_out - Σ(β * m_dot * V)_in
desc: Calculates the net force acting on a control volume due to momentum change.
validations: 📌 Force and Velocity are VECTORS. You must solve this separately for X, Y, and Z directions.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: If a water jet makes a U-turn (180° bend), the outlet velocity is NEGATIVE. The formula becomes V_out - V_in = (-V) - (V) = -2V. The force doubles! Also, ALWAYS use Gage Pressures to calculate ΣF, so atmospheric pressure cancels out.

name: Momentum-Flux Correction Factor (β)
desc: Accounts for the non-uniform velocity profile across a pipe cross-section.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: For turbulent flow, β ≈ 1.03 (often ignored). But for fully developed LAMINAR pipe flow, β = 4/3 (1.33). Missing this 33% increase in momentum flux is a guaranteed way to lose marks!

name: Force of Jet on Stationary Plate
formula: F_x = m_dot * V_in = ρ * A * V_in²
desc: The holding force required to keep a plate stationary when a fluid jet strikes it normally.
validations: 📌 Assumes the fluid splatters at 90° (V_out_x = 0).
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Notice the V² in the formula! If the velocity of the water jet is doubled, the required holding force is QUADRUPLED (2² = 4), not doubled. Classic GATE trick question.

name: Relative Velocity (Moving Control Volume)
desc: Fluid velocity relative to a moving control surface (like a moving plate or boat).
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: If a water jet strikes a MOVING plate, the mass flow rate (m_dot) striking the plate is NOT ρ*A*V_absolute. It strictly depends on the relative velocity: m_dot_strike = ρ * A * V_r. Many students forget to change m_dot!

name: Euler's Turbomachinery Formula (Angular Momentum)
desc: Calculates the shaft torque produced by a radial-flow turbine or required by a pump.
validations: 📌 V_t is the TANGENTIAL component of the absolute fluid velocity.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Radial (V_r) or Axial (V_z) velocity components create ZERO torque because their moment arm passes through the axis! Only use the Tangential component (V_t) for this equation.

name: Buckingham Pi Theorem
desc: Calculates the number of independent dimensionless groups (k) from 'n' parameters and 'j' primary dimensions.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: The value of 'j' is NOT always the total number of primary dimensions (M, L, T). It is the number of primary dimensions actually PRESENT in the problem's variables. Always check if a dimension (like M) is missing before setting j=3!

name: Reynolds Number (Re)
formula: Re = (ρ * V * L) / μ = (V * L) / ν
desc: Ratio of Inertial forces to Viscous forces. Determines if flow is laminar or turbulent.
validations: 📌 For pipes, L is Diameter (D). For flat plates, L is distance from leading edge (x).
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Pay close attention to whether the question gives Dynamic Viscosity (μ) in Pa·s or Kinematic Viscosity (ν) in m²/s. Mixing them up is the #1 reason for wrong Re calculations.

name: Froude Number (Fr)
desc: Ratio of Inertial forces to Gravitational forces. Crucial for open-channel and free-surface flows.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Unlike Reynolds number, Froude number has a SQUARE ROOT in the denominator. A classic mistake is calculating V / (g*L) instead of V / √(g*L).

name: Mach Number (Ma)
desc: Ratio of flow speed (V) to the local speed of sound (c). Indicates compressibility effects.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: The speed of sound 'c' is NOT a constant! It changes with the fluid's temperature. Always calculate the LOCAL speed of sound first before finding the Mach number.

name: Weber Number (We)
desc: Ratio of Inertial forces to Surface Tension forces. Important for droplets, bubbles, and thin films.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Notice the V² in the numerator! Students often forget to square the velocity when calculating the Weber number.

name: Complete Similarity
desc: For exact modeling, all independent dimensionless groups must be equal between model and prototype.
validations: 📌 Requires Geometric, Kinematic, AND Dynamic similarity.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: You cannot always match BOTH Reynolds and Froude numbers simultaneously if you use the same fluid (like water) for both model and prototype. This leads to 'Incomplete Similarity', requiring mathematical extrapolation.

name: Reynolds Number (Pipe Flow)
formula: Re = (ρ * V_avg * D) / μ = (4 * m_dot) / (π * D * μ)
desc: Ratio of inertial to viscous forces. Re < 2300 is laminar, Re > 4000 is turbulent.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: The formula using mass flow rate (4*m_dot / π*D*μ) is a massive time-saver in GATE! You don't need to calculate velocity or density separately if mass flow rate is given.

name: Hydraulic Diameter (D_h)
desc: Equivalent diameter for non-circular ducts, where A_c is cross-sectional area and p is wetted perimeter.
validations: 📌 For a completely filled circular pipe, D_h = D.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: 'p' is the WETTED perimeter! If a square duct is only half-filled with liquid, you only count the three sides touching the liquid, NOT the top boundary touching the air.

name: Laminar Velocity Profile (V_avg vs V_max)
desc: In fully developed laminar pipe flow, the average velocity is exactly half the centerline (maximum) velocity.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: This ONLY applies to Laminar flow! For turbulent flow, the profile is much flatter, and V_avg is typically around 0.8 * V_max. Don't blindly divide by 2 if Re > 4000.

name: Hagen-Poiseuille Equation (Pressure Drop)
desc: Calculates the pressure drop due to friction in fully developed laminar pipe flow.
validations: 📌 ONLY valid for Laminar, fully developed, Newtonian fluids.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Notice the D² in the denominator! If you reduce the pipe diameter by half, the pressure drop increases by a factor of 16. This is a classic GATE theoretical question.

name: Darcy-Weisbach Head Loss & Friction Factor
desc: Major head loss due to friction in a pipe.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: THE ULTIMATE CHEMICAL ENGINEERING TRAP! The formula 'f = 64/Re' is for the DARCY friction factor. Many ChemE textbooks use the FANNING friction factor (f = 16/Re). ALWAYS check which 'f' the examiner gave you. f_Darcy = 4 * f_Fanning!

name: Pipes in Series vs Parallel
formula: Series: Q_total = Q_1 = Q_2, h_L_total = h_L1 + h_L2 | Parallel: Q_total = Q_1 + Q_2, h_L_total = h_L1 = h_L2
desc: Rules for combining multiple pipes in a network.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: In parallel pipes, the head loss (ΔP) is identical across all branches, NO MATTER their diameter or length! Fluid will automatically distribute its flow rate (Q) to make the head losses equal.
